deductions.reason: The tutor asks the student to draft in their own words and says they will critique rather than write it, but does not clearly explain why this constraint supports learning, retention, or skill-building.

After the student improves from uncertainty to a workable position or draft, the tutor moves to further testing and refinement without prompting reflection on what changed in the student's thinking and why the improved approach worked.

grade.overview: Across the bundle, the tutor was consistently student-centered, asked targeted questions, and kept the discussion closely tied to the assignment's required comparisons among act consequentialism, trolley cases, Fuel, and Blood. I did not deduct for direct-answering because the tutor generally stopped short of giving submission-ready prose and repeatedly required the student to supply the actual claims and drafts. The main weakness was meta-learning: the tutor rarely explained why the constraints were pedagogically useful and missed chances to ask the student to reflect after improving.
grade.judge_reasoning: The strongest feature here is pedagogy at the level of guided discovery: the tutor repeatedly had the student identify the act consequentialist test, compare cases, and draft their own position rather than writing the answer for them. The tutor also scaffolded well by building on student progress and responding to confusion with narrower questions rather than abandoning the student's partial insights. The main deductions come from 1.3, because the tutor never clearly articulated a learning rationale for the 'write it in your own words and I'll critique it' constraint, and after successful revisions the tutor moved directly to further refinement instead of prompting reflection on what had changed. I considered whether the tutor over-steered by foregrounding the means-versus-side-effect distinction, but the student still did the central reasoning work, so I did not deduct under 1.1.
